macro_segments.title: The Constitutive Role of Witnesses in Kiddushin: Can One Witness Suffice with Mutual Agreement?

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Central Question: Witnesses as Essential to Kiddushin, Not Just Proof
The Teaching of Rav: One Witness Cannot Effectuate Kiddushin Even with Mutual Consent
Rabbah bar Rav Huna's Formulation: The Great Beis Din's Position on Single Witness
Variant Readings: Attribution to Rav via Different Chains of Transmission

macro_segments.title: The Case of Two Men and a Woman with Disputed Claims: Testing Witness Requirements Without Objective Evidence

micro_segments.title: The Scenario: Two Men and a Woman Arriving with a Bundle Each Claiming Different Ownership
The Halachic Ruling: Two Gitin Required to Collect Kesuvah from the Bundle
Clarifying the Case: Witnesses Present to Marry, Not Contradicting Claims
The Chiddush: Rabbi Meir's View on Collecting Kesuvah from Movable Property

macro_segments.title: The Debate on Single Unopposed Witness in Personal Status Matters: Abaye versus Rava

micro_segments.title: Contrasting Positions: Rav Kahana Permits, Rav Papa Concerned About Single Witness Kiddushin
Rav Ashi's Challenge: Deriving the Gezeirah Shavah from Monetary and Personal Status Texts
The Distinction Between Edei Beirur and Edei Kiyum: Witnesses for Proof versus Witnesses for Establishment

macro_segments.title: Monetary Matters: Witnesses as Proof Only, Not Constitutive; Admission Works in Absence of Opposition

micro_segments.title: Mar Zutra and Rav Ada Saba's Question: Does Dividing Assets Without Witnesses Require Repetition?
Abaye's Answer: Witnesses Were Created Only to Prevent Liars (Edei Beirur)
Conceptual Framework: Edei Beirur versus Edei Kiyum as Two Distinct Roles of Testimony

macro_segments.title: Single Unopposed Witness in Matters of Purity, Korban Obligation, and Animal Status

micro_segments.title: Contamination of Grain: One Unopposed Witness Renders Terumah Impure
Obligation of Korban: One Unopposed Witness for Eating Forbidden Fat (Chelev)
Animal Disqualification: One Witness for Bestiality or Murder by Animal
The Tzbura: Why All Three Cases Are Necessary—Silence as Admission in Context
Tosafos's Analysis: Silence as Admission Only When It Causes Personal Loss to the Owner

macro_segments.title: Wife's Alleged Adultery: Abaye's Position That Unopposed Single Witness Can Require Divorce

micro_segments.title: The Case: One Witness Claims Wife Committed Adultery; She Is Silent
Abaye Says Believed; Rava Says Not Believed: Debate on Single Witness in Davar She'be'ervah
Reframing the Debate: Not Silence as Admission, But Reliability of Single Witness in Personal Status

macro_segments.title: The Blind Man and the Messenger: Abaye's Source for Trusting a Single Unopposed Witness in Marital Matters

micro_segments.title: The Setup: Blind Scholar's Delayed Arrival, Messenger's Observation of Wife's Adultery
Shmuel's Ruling: If You Trust This Messenger as Trustworthy, Act on His Report
Abaye's Proof: Single Unopposed Witness Can Obligate Divorce Even Without Husband's Presence
Rava's Alternative Reading: Shmuel Requires Implicit Trust Equivalent to Two Witnesses

macro_segments.title: The Story of King Yannai, the Pharisees, and the Crisis of Torah Authority: Historical Backdrop to the Single Witness Debate

micro_segments.title: The Feast: Yannai's Celebration After Military Conquest, Elazar ben Poira's Insinuation
The Insult: Yehudah ben Gedidya Challenges Yannai's Right to the High Priesthood
The Rumor: Yannai's Mother Allegedly Taken Captive, Investigation Found Nothing
The Conspiracy: Elazar Exploits Yannai's Insecurity, Orchestrates Massacre of Sages
The Heresy: Dismissing Oral Torah, the Written Law Suffices; Restoration by Shimon ben Shetach

macro_segments.title: Analysis of the Yannai Story: Evidence Regarding Yannai's Mother's Captivity and Personal Status

micro_segments.title: The Question: How Was the Rumor About Yannai's Mother Disproven?
Two Against Two or One Against One: Determining What Evidence Existed and How It Was Dismissed
Rashi's Approach: Child's Status Remains in Doubt When Two Witnesses Conflict on Mother's Status
The Gemara's Inference: Single Unopposed Witness Would Have Been Believed in Davar She'be'ervah
Rava's Counter: Witnesses Were Two Against Two; Dismissed via Hazamah (Impeachment), Not Lack of Effect
Alternative Explanation: The Two Witnesses Provided Complementary Information, Not Contradiction
